openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-rgs7x container=linuxptp-daemon-container,

err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded

```

From the GCS JUnit XML (full fetch), all 3 testcases carry the same Timed out after 202.071s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig test-slave1 failure message.


Daemon pod rgs7x was healthy during the entire window — confirmed from its pod log artifact on node cnfdf32:

```

[linuxptp-daemon-rgs7x] 2026-07-07T03:42:51.929962 daemon.go:389] Ptp4lConf: #profile: test-slave1

[linuxptp-daemon-rgs7x] 2026-07-07T03:42:51.929973 message_tag [ptp4l.0.config:{level}]

[linuxptp-daemon-rgs7x] 2026-07-07T03:43:00.018Z ptp4l: [ptp4l.0.config:6] master offset -4 s2 freq +6884 path delay 1173

[linuxptp-daemon-rgs7x] 2026-07-07T03:44:59.845Z ptp4l: [ptp4l.0.config:6] master offset -7 s2 freq +6937 path delay 1179

```

State s2 (locked), master offset consistently under 20 ns between 03:43–03:45. The daemon logged the exact Ptp4lConf: #profile: test-slave1 … message_tag [ptp4l.0.config:{level}] pattern the test regex requires (ptphelper.go:85: (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: %s(.|\n)?message_tag \[(.*)\]) — only at startup (03:42:51). Pass 1 of GetPodLogsRegex (pods.go:215–226) opens a no-follow log stream with a context.WithTimeout(…, TimeoutIn1Minute), which expired before the API server returned the log content.


The same timeout pattern spans every suite run during the 3.5-hour window: bc (04:58), dualnicbc (04:04), dualnicbcha (04:33), oc (05:24), tbc (02:52), tgm (02:18) — 22 total failures across 7 suites all reducing to the same context deadline exceeded or timedout waiting for matches in the Kubernetes pod-log streaming call.


• Root Cause: The Kubernetes API server's pod-log streaming endpoint was degraded for the duration of this job run. GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:84) issues a CoreV1().Pods().GetLogs().Stream(ctx) call with a 1-minute context deadline (pods.go:220). On every suite — from 02:18 to 05:44 — that call either timed out completely (context deadline exceeded) or established a stream that never delivered the startup-phase Ptp4lConf: log lines (timedout waiting for matches). The PTP daemon, hardware sync, and network topology were all functioning correctly; the failure is entirely in the CI cluster's API server log-streaming layer.

• Breaking PR/Commit: Unknown — no code change in ptp-operator main or linuxptp-daemon release-4.21 is implicated. The deployed daemon commit 2868ef28681d4a3b2e52bbefbe7e7b66e82897e0 was producing correct output and correct sync. No backport verification is required because the root cause is infrastructure, not a cherry-pick gap.

• Is it a flake? Yes — transient CI infrastructure degradation (API server log-streaming unavailable or severely throttled across the entire job run). No code regression in test or daemon.

• Suggested Fix: Re-run the job; if the pattern recurs on multiple nightly runs, file a CI infrastructure ticket about API server log-streaming latency on the telco5g cluster. As a test-code hardening measure, consider increasing TimeoutIn1Minute to 3–5 minutes in GetPodLogsRegex Pass 1, or caching the Ptp4lConf profile ID from the first successful parse rather than re-streaming it for every test.

Suite Issue Class: infra_other

Suite Severity: MEDIUM


dualnicbc



Suite: dualnicbc (3 failures)


Suite TL;DR: All 3 dualnicbc ClockSync test failures share the identical root cause as 22 failures spanning every other PTP suite (bc, dualfollower, dualnicbcha, oc, tbc, tgm) — PTP clock synchronization never reached the LOCKED state during the run, indicating a lab-wide hardware/grandmaster availability issue.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: PTP clock never reached LOCKED state — all sync-dependent tests timed out (affects: all 3 dualnicbc tests: "Slave can sync to master", "Downstream slave can sync to BC master", "Can provide a profile with higher priority")
• Evidence:

```

junit-failures.txt (all 3 dualnicbc entries):

Test: [It] [dualnicbc-serial] PTP e2e tests PTP ClockSync Slave can sync to master [Serial]

Message: result: error

Test: [It] [dualnicbc-serial] PTP e2e tests PTP ClockSync Downstream slave can sync to BC master [Serial]

Message: result: error

Test: [It] [dualnicbc-serial] PTP e2e tests PTP ClockSync Can provide a profile with higher priority [Serial]

Message: result: error


test_results.json summary:

{"total": 354, "pass": 160, "skip": 172, "fail": 0, "error": 22, ...}


The identical "result: error" appears for the same ClockSync tests

a




TL;DR: A single test-code timeout regression blocks clock-sync validation in 20/22 tests; 2 remaining failures expose a known DPLL holdover gap.



Root Cause Groups:


• 20/22 failures — GetProfileLogID log-stream snapshot times out (test regression, commit 41f3dd9):

Suites bc (3), dualfollower (3), dualnicbc (3), dualnicbcha (3), oc (2), tbc (6).

Commit 41f3dd9bf0b33f642d931eb005e54eba89b337fa ("address gemini review", Feb 26 2026) silently reduced the GetPodLogsRegex snapshot context from TimeoutIn3Minutes to TimeoutIn1Minute. By the time any suite runs (the linuxptp-daemon has been up for 3–5 hours), the container's accumulated log volume cannot be streamed within 1 minute; io.ReadAll returns context deadline exceeded. The fallback follow-stream also fails because the Ptp4lConf: #profile: marker was written only at daemon startup and does not reappear. All six affected suites produce the same GetProfileLogID attempt N failed … error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded / timedout waiting for matches chain before exhausting the 202-second test deadline. The tbc suite (6 failures) has a compounding factor: linuxptp-daemon-t68bh on cnfdf32 is also crash-looping (suspected duplicate DiscoveredFollowerInterfaces=[ens7f0 ens7f0] config), which kills active streams mid-read and amplifies the timeout.


• 2/22 failures — DPLL holdover collapses to FREERUN (class 248) without sustaining class 7 (known tech debt):

Suite tgm (2).

In linuxptp-daemon/pkg/dpll/dpll.go:656, the DPLL_HOLDOVER branch immediately checks !d.inSpec; if inSpec was cleared during the preceding DPLL_FREERUN transition, the daemon fires holdoverCloseCh and falls to class 248 without ever holding class 7. The code carries an explicit // TODO: GNSS holdover currently doesn't offer holdover out of spec comment. Both TGM holdover tests fail deterministically on cnfdf32.



Cross-Suite Patterns:


Root CauseSuites affectedSuite-specific?
GetProfileLogID 1-min timeout regressionbc, dualfollower, dualnicbc, dualnicbcha, oc, tbcNo — all 6 suites hit identical error path
tbc crash-loop on cnfdf32 (compounding)tbc onlyYes — specific to T-BC node config
DPLL holdover tech debttgm onlyYes — only holdover tests exercise this path

All suites share the identical ptptesthelper.go:71 → ptphelper.go:123 → pods.go:220 call chain. The oc suite provided the most complete trace, isolating 41f3dd9 as the breaking commit. The dualnicbc per-suite analysis incorrectly attributed the cause to hardware/GM unavailability; the actual mechanism is the same log-stream timeout seen everywhere else.



Actionable Recommendations:


1. Revert or fix commit 41f3dd9 in GetPodLogsRegex (highest impact — fixes 20/22 failures):

In test/pkg/pods/pods.go (around line 220), restore the snapshot context timeout from TimeoutIn1Minute back to TimeoutIn3Minutes (as set by the prior commit 9c1bc7f). Alternatively, redesign GetProfileLogID to search only the log tail (e.g., sinceSeconds or tailLines parameter on the Kubernetes log request) rather than re-reading the full accumulated log, making the lookup O(recent output) regardless of daemon uptime.


2. Investigate and fix the linuxptp-daemon crash-loop on cnfdf32 (fixes tbc amplification):

The DiscoveredFollowerInterfaces=[ens7f0 ens7f0] duplicate entry in the T-BC config suggests a profile-discovery bug where the same interface is registered twice. Confirm whether test-wpc-tbc profile configuration is correct for node cnfdf32.telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com, and fix the tbc-tr/tbc-tt interface mapping to prevent the crash-loop that makes log-stream reads impossible even with a corrected timeout.


3. Address the DPLL holdover tech debt in linuxptp-daemon/pkg/dpll/dpll.go:656 (fixes tgm failures):

Implement the deferred holdover-in-spec window so that after GNSS loss the daemon sustains ClockClass7 for the holdover duration before falling to class 248, mirroring the T-BC holdover behavior called out in the existing TODO comment.



Overall Issue Class: test_regression (dominant — 20/22 failures caused by 41f3dd9 test-code change; tgm failures are test_failure against known gap; tbc crash-loop is infra_other)


Overall Severity: HIGH — a single test-code commit broke clock-sync verification across every PTP topology for the entire job, producing 20 false-negative failures that mask any real regressions in the 4.21 PTP stack.




Per-Suite Details



bc



Suite: bc (3 failures)


Suite TL;DR: All three ClockSync tests fail identically because GetProfileLogID cannot find the Ptp4lConf: #profile: log entry in the linuxptp-daemon pod's log stream, causing the master-clock-ID lookup to time out before sync can be verified.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: Profile log-ID lookup failure — regex mismatch/log rollover (affects: all 3 failed tests)
• Evidence:
• Test 1 (Slave can sync to master, ptpconfig test-bc-master1):

```

[FAILED] Timed out after 202.066s.

Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig test-bc-master1

could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for

openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-8mfx6 container=linuxptp-daemon-container,

err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded

In [It] at: .../ptptesthelper/ptptesthelper.go:71

```

• Test 2 (Downstream slave can sync to BC master, ptpconfig test-slave1):

```

[FAILED] Timed out after 202.446s.

Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig test-slave1

could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for

openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-xxnzl container=linuxptp-daemon-container,

err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded

```

• Test 3 (Can provide a profile with higher priority, ptpconfig temp):

```

[FAILED] Timed out after 201.666s.

Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig temp

could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for

openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-8mfx6 container=linuxptp-daemon-container,

err=timedout waiting for matches

```

• All three hit the same Eventually block at ptptesthelper.go:71 with a ~202 s timeout (= pkg.TimeoutIn3Minutes).
• The same failure pattern repeats across all 7 test suites (22 failures total: bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc, dualnicbcha, oc, tbc, tgm), confirming this is systemic.
• From build log: job ran against PTP_UNDER_TEST_BRANCH=release-4.21; cluster setup and config-verification tests all passed; 6 nodes Ready.

• Root Cause: BasicClockSyncCheck (in ptptesthelper.go:71) wraps ptphelper.GetClockIDForeign(profileName, …) in a 3-minute Eventually loop. GetClockIDForeign calls GetProfileLogID, which searches the linuxptp-daemon pod log stream for the regex (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: (.|\n)?message_tag \[(.)\] (in ptphelper.go:85). The profileName passed here is the return value of GetProfileName(ptpConfig, true) (ptphelper.go:966), which since commit 84723c8 uses QualifyProfileName(config.Name, profile.Name) to prefix the profile name with the CRD name. Because the CRD name and profile name are identical for standard test configs (e.g., CRD test-bc-master1, profile test-bc-master1), QualifyProfileName returns "test-bc-master1_test-bc-master1". The daemon (release-4.21) logs Ptp4lConf: #profile: test-bc-master1 (from daemon.go:1131, output.profile_name = nodeProfile.Name, using the raw spec profile name). The doubled suffix never appears in the log stream, so both Pass 1 (existing-log snapshot) and Pass 2 (follow stream) of GetPodLogsRegex fail. The /var/run/ fallback added by the same commit (GetConfigForProfileFromVarRun, ptphelper.go:524) also searches for #profile:\stest-bc-master1_test-bc-master1 in config files that only contain #profile: test-bc-master1, so it too fails. GetProfileLogID returns error; GetClockIDForeign returns immediately without attempting the pmc fallback; the Eventually at ptptesthelper.go:71 exhausts its 3-minute budget.

• Breaking PR/Commit: 84723c8 (test: Add T-BC CI tests and configuration, 2026-03-25, author: nocturnalastro) — introduced QualifyProfileName and changed GetProfileName from returning profile.Name directly (unqualified) to returning QualifyProfileName(config.Name, profile.Name), causing a doubled-prefix regex for all profiles where CRD name equals profile name. See test/pkg/ptphelper/ptphelper.go:952–961 and 966–993. Commit is on k8snetworkplumbingwg/ptp-operator@main; the test binary runs from main per TEST_BRANCH=main in the build log.

• Is it a flake? No. The regex Ptp4lConf: #profile: test-bc-master1_test-bc-master1 will deterministically never match daemon logs that only ever emit #profile: test-bc-master1. The failure reproduces identically across all three bc tests and all other suites in the same run.

• Suggested Fix: In GetProfileName (ptphelper.go:966), revert to returning *profile.Name for the existing standard test profiles (bc-master, slave, temp, etc.) rather than calling QualifyProfileName. Reserve qualification only for multi-profile CRDs where the CRD name differs from the profile name (i.e., the T-BC case with tbc-tr/tbc-tt). Alternatively, fix QualifyProfileName to detect when crName == profileName and return it unmodified, avoiding the double-prefix.

Suite Issue Class: test_regression

Suite Severity: HIGH


dualfollower



Suite: dualfollower (3 failures)


Suite TL;DR: All 3 ClockSync failures share one root cause — the Kubernetes pod-log streaming API timed out when the test tried to read the linuxptp-daemon profile configuration from pod linuxptp-daemon-rgs7x, despite that pod being properly PTP-synced throughout the window.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: Kubernetes log-streaming timeout in GetProfileLogID (affects: Slave can sync to master, Dual follower can sync when one follower port goes down, Can provide a profile with higher priority)
• Evidence:

```

[Jul 7 03:44:29.442][ptphelper.go: 123] GetProfileLogID attempt 1 failed for test-slave1_test-slave1:

could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for

openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-rgs7x container=linuxptp-daemon-container,

err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded, retrying...


[Jul 7 03:45:39.670][ptphelper.go: 123] GetProfileLogID attempt 2 failed for test-slave1_test-slave1:

could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for

openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-rgs7x container=linuxptp-daemon-container,

err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded, retrying...


[Jul 7 03:46:50.495][ptptesthelper.go: 68] GetClockIDForeign retry due to err:

could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for

openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-rgs7x container=linuxptp-daemon-container,

err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded

```

From the GCS JUnit XML (full fetch), all 3 testcases carry the same Timed out after 202.071s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig test-slave1 failure message.


Daemon pod rgs7x was healthy during the entire window — confirmed from its pod log artifact on node cnfdf32:

```

[linuxptp-daemon-rgs7x] 2026-07-07T03:42:51.929962 daemon.go:389] Ptp4lConf: #profile: test-slave1

[linuxptp-daemon-rgs7x] 2026-07-07T03:42:51.929973 message_tag [ptp4l.0.config:{level}]

[linuxptp-daemon-rgs7x] 2026-07-07T03:43:00.018Z ptp4l: [ptp4l.0.config:6] master offset -4 s2 freq +6884 path delay 1173

[linuxptp-daemon-rgs7x] 2026-07-07T03:44:59.845Z ptp4l: [ptp4l.0.config:6] master offset -7 s2 freq +6937 path delay 1179

```

State s2 (locked), master offset consistently under 20 ns between 03:43–03:45. The daemon logged the exact Ptp4lConf: #profile: test-slave1 … message_tag [ptp4l.0.config:{level}] pattern the test regex requires (ptphelper.go:85: (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: %s(.|\n)?message_tag \[(.*)\]) — only at startup (03:42:51). Pass 1 of GetPodLogsRegex (pods.go:215–226) opens a no-follow log stream with a context.WithTimeout(…, TimeoutIn1Minute), which expired before the API server returned the log content.


The same timeout pattern spans every suite run during the 3.5-hour window: bc (04:58), dualnicbc (04:04), dualnicbcha (04:33), oc (05:24), tbc (02:52), tgm (02:18) — 22 total failures across 7 suites all reducing to the same context deadline exceeded or timedout waiting for matches in the Kubernetes pod-log streaming call.


• Root Cause: The Kubernetes API server's pod-log streaming endpoint was degraded for the duration of this job run. GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:84) issues a CoreV1().Pods().GetLogs().Stream(ctx) call with a 1-minute context deadline (pods.go:220). On every suite — from 02:18 to 05:44 — that call either timed out completely (context deadline exceeded) or established a stream that never delivered the startup-phase Ptp4lConf: log lines (timedout waiting for matches). The PTP daemon, hardware sync, and network topology were all functioning correctly; the failure is entirely in the CI cluster's API server log-streaming layer.

• Breaking PR/Commit: Unknown — no code change in ptp-operator main or linuxptp-daemon release-4.21 is implicated. The deployed daemon commit 2868ef28681d4a3b2e52bbefbe7e7b66e82897e0 was producing correct output and correct sync. No backport verification is required because the root cause is infrastructure, not a cherry-pick gap.

• Is it a flake? Yes — transient CI infrastructure degradation (API server log-streaming unavailable or severely throttled across the entire job run). No code regression in test or daemon.

• Suggested Fix: Re-run the job; if the pattern recurs on multiple nightly runs, file a CI infrastructure ticket about API server log-streaming latency on the telco5g cluster. As a test-code hardening measure, consider increasing TimeoutIn1Minute to 3–5 minutes in GetPodLogsRegex Pass 1, or caching the Ptp4lConf profile ID from the first successful parse rather than re-streaming it for every test.

Suite Issue Class: infra_other

Suite Severity: MEDIUM


dualnicbc



Suite: dualnicbc (3 failures)


Suite TL;DR: All 3 dualnicbc ClockSync test failures share the identical root cause as 22 failures spanning every other PTP suite (bc, dualfollower, dualnicbcha, oc, tbc, tgm) — PTP clock synchronization never reached the LOCKED state during the run, indicating a lab-wide hardware/grandmaster availability issue.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: PTP clock never reached LOCKED state — all sync-dependent tests timed out (affects: all 3 dualnicbc tests: "Slave can sync to master", "Downstream slave can sync to BC master", "Can provide a profile with higher priority")
• Evidence:

```

junit-failures.txt (all 3 dualnicbc entries):

Test: [It] [dualnicbc-serial] PTP e2e tests PTP ClockSync Slave can sync to master [Serial]

Message: result: error

Test: [It] [dualnicbc-serial] PTP e2e tests PTP ClockSync Downstream slave can sync to BC master [Serial]

Message: result: error

Test: [It] [dualnicbc-serial] PTP e2e tests PTP ClockSync Can provide a profile with higher priority [Serial]

Message: result: error


test_results.json summary:

{"total": 354, "pass": 160, "skip": 172, "fail": 0, "error": 22, ...}


The identical "result: error" appears for the same ClockSync tests

a




TL;DR: TBC T-BC discovery is broken (6/9 failures); holdover flakiness and a phc2sys HA timeout account for the rest.


Root Cause Groups:


• 6/9 failures — T-BC discovery broken (GetInterfaces reads only Profile[0]): All 6 tbc suite tests fail because api/v1/ptpconfig_webhook.go::GetInterfaces unconditionally indexes config.Spec.Profile[0], leaving masterIfCount: 0 and DiscoveredMasterInterfaces=[] for the two-profile test-wpc-tbc PtpConfig. Introduced by ptp-operator PR #205 (merged 2026-06-23) exposing a pre-existing bug in the webhook.

• 2/9 failures — WPC T-GM holdover not reached/sustained (flaky hardware on cnfdf32): Both tgm-serial tests fail because the WPC T-GM on cnfdf32.telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com either never enters clock class 7 within 3 minutes, or flashes through class 7 so briefly the test cannot observe it before reverting to class 248 (FREERUN). Likely a near-zero LocalHoldoverTimeout or unreliable GNSS-disable simulation on this node's firmware.

• 1/9 failures — phc2sys HA failover timeout (dualnicbcha): phc2sys does not emit the secondary-interface selection log line within 60 s after ens2f1 is taken down; GetPodLogsRegexSince hits context deadline exceeded. Timing-sensitive; possibly a regression from linuxptp-daemon upstream sync commit fd6ff1e (2026-06-30), but unconfirmed.

Cross-Suite Patterns:


• Suite-specific: The tbc failure is entirely self-contained — a code bug in the webhook that no other suite triggers.
• Shared pattern (tbc Group 2 + dualnicbcha): Both tbc secondary failures and the dualnicbcha failure share the same surface symptom — context deadline exceeded from GetPodLogsRegexSince/log-stream polling — but for different underlying reasons (broken topology vs. slow daemon response). Not the same root cause.
• tgm is hardware-environment-specific: The holdover failures are isolated to one physical node (cnfdf32) and do not overlap with the software-side failures in the other suites.

Actionable Recommendations:


1. Fix GetInterfaces in api/v1/ptpconfig_webhook.go — iterate over all config.Spec.Profile[:] entries (not just [0]), aggregating master/slave interfaces by ptpRole. This is a one-line loop change that immediately unblocks all 6 TBC test failures (67% of the job).

2. Investigate cnfdf32 T-GM hardware configuration — check the LocalHoldoverTimeout value in the WPC HardwareConfig CRD on that node, and verify that ubxtool -z CFG-NAVSPG-INFIL_NCNOTHRS,50,1 reliably simulates GNSS loss on the installed firmware version. If the holdover window is hardware-limited, extend test polling budgets or skip on nodes known to have short holdover.

3. Add a passing-baseline check for the phc2sys HA failover test — instrument or log the time phc2sys takes to emit the secondary-interface selection after primary link-down, and compare against the 60 s timeout to determine whether the timeout needs extending or the linuxptp-daemon upstream sync introduced a regression in HA switching speed.

Overall Issue Class: test_regression (primary: code bug in webhook exposed by new TBC tests) + test_flake (secondary: hardware holdover and timing sensitivity)


Overall Severity: HIGH — 6 of 9 failures have a known, fixable root cause with a clear code change; the remaining 3 are environmental/timing-dependent.




Per-Suite Details



dualnicbcha



Suite: dualnicbcha (1 failures)


Suite TL;DR: phc2sys failed to emit a secondary-interface selection log line within the 60-second timeout after the primary BC slave interface (ens2f1) was deliberately taken down, causing the HA failover verification step to hit context deadline exceeded.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: phc2sys HA failover did not complete within test timeout (affects: [It] [dualnicbcha-serial] PTP e2e tests PTP ClockSync DualNICBCHA phc2sys switches to secondary ptp4l when primary interface fails [Serial])

• Evidence:

```

INFO [Jul 7 14:58:22.906][ptp.go: 1103] phc2sys log matching line: phc2sys[18333.639]: [phc2sys.2.config:6] selecting ens2f1 as out-of-domain source clock

INFO [Jul 7 14:58:25.557][ptptesthelper.go: 435] Turning interface: ens2f1 in pod ptp-network-outage-recovery-tl8kz down, stdout: , stderr:

STEP: Verifying phc2sys switches to a different interface - ptp.go:1125 @ 07/07/26 14:58:30.816

[FAILED] Unexpected error: could not find regex in log stream for openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-jz5vh container=linuxptp-daemon-container, err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded

In [It] at: /tmp/cnf-8b4Nw/ptp-operator-conformance-test/test/conformance/serial/ptp.go:1130 @ 07/07/26 14:59:30.817

```

Test ran for 73s total; the GetPodLogsRegexSince call watching for phc2sys(?m).?:. selecting (\w+) as out-of-domain source clock (at ptp.go:1125) exhausted pkg.TimeoutIn1Minute (60s) without observing the secondary interface being selected.


• Root Cause: phc2sys was correctly using ens2f1 as the out-of-domain clock source initially (HA discovery successful). After ens2f1 was forced down, phc2sys should switch to the secondary BC ptp4l socket (ens4f2) and emit a new selecting ... as out-of-domain source clock log entry. The switch did not produce that log line within 60 seconds. The delay is timing-sensitive: it depends on how long ptp4l on the primary socket takes to enter HOLDOVER/FREERUN and how quickly phc2sys's HA source selection responds. The test assertion at ptp.go:1130 calls Expect(err).NotTo(HaveOccurred()) on the return of pods.GetPodLogsRegexSince — when the stream context deadline expires before any regex match, err is non-nil and the test fails. Note: the WARNING ptp4l conf parsing failed, err=config option not in section for test-dual-nic-bc-ha is expected (the HA profile has no standalone ptp4l config body) and does not affect runtime behavior.

• Breaking PR/Commit: Possibly linuxptp-daemon PR #617 (fd6ff1e, merged 2026-06-30, "CNF-22056, CNF-22693, CNF-25167, OCPBUGS-85586, OCPBUGS-93744: Sync from upstream (30-Jun-2026)") — this was a large initial upstream sync (added pkg/daemon/daemon.go, log_parsing.go, and the full daemon codebase in a single commit one week before this run). However, without a history of prior passing runs to compare against, this cannot be confirmed as the causal change. Breaking PR/Commit: Unknown (possible fd6ff1e in k8snetworkplumbingwg/linuxptp-daemon).

• Is it a flake? Yes. The HA switch itself was architecturally working (phc2sys correctly selected the primary interface on startup). The failure is a timing race between the hardware/kernel reacting to the link-down event, ptp4l entering HOLDOVER, phc2sys detecting the source degradation, and the 60-second test deadline. This test has historically been susceptible to this pattern on real hardware.

• Suggested Fix: Retry the job to confirm flakiness. If it recurs, increase pkg.TimeoutIn1Minute for the GetPodLogsRegexSince call at ptp.go:1125 to a longer window (e.g., 2–3 minutes), or investigate whether PR #617 changed phc2sys HA source-selection timing (e.g., holdover duration or polling interval) in the k8snetworkplumbingwg/linuxptp-daemon main branch.

Suite Issue Class: test_flake

Suite Severity: LOW


tbc



Suite: tbc (6 failures)


Suite TL;DR: All 6 TBC tests fail because the test-wpc-tbc PtpConfig is discovered with masterIfCount: 0 and no downstream slave PtpConfig, caused by a bug in api/v1/ptpconfig_webhook.go::GetInterfaces that only reads Profile[0] of a multi-profile T-BC config — leaving the TBC unable to sync and the test topology incomplete.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: T-BC discovery broken — GetInterfaces reads only Profile[0] (affects: all 6 tests)
• Evidence:

```

WARNING[Jul 7 13:24:41.891][ptpconfig_webhook.go: 457] More than one profile detected for ptpconfig test-wpc-tbc

INFO[testconfig.go: 2172] ptptConfig: test-wpc-tbc, masterIfCount: 0, slaveIfCount: 1

INFO[testconfig.go: 2207] Detected T-BC configuration with tbc-tr and tbc-tt profiles

INFO[testconfig.go: 940] ...DiscoveredSlave1PtpConfig= nil, DiscoveredMasterInterfaces=[]DiscoveredFollowerInterfaces=[ens7f0 ens7f0]...

```

• Root Cause: api/v1/ptpconfig_webhook.go line 455, function GetInterfaces, unconditionally uses config.Spec.Profile[0] even when a PtpConfig has multiple profiles. The T-BC config test-wpc-tbc has two profiles: tbc-tr (upstream slave) at Profile[0] and tbc-tt (downstream master) at Profile[1]. Because GetInterfaces ignores Profile[1], masterIfCount is always 0 for any T-BC config. The warning More than one profile detected fires at line 457 but the function proceeds anyway. This leaves DiscoveredMasterInterfaces=[] and DiscoveredSlave1PtpConfig=nil throughout all TBC tests. T-BC tests were introduced in ptp-operator PR #205 merged 2026-06-23 on main — two weeks before this job ran — and appear never to have passed end-to-end due to this bug.
• Breaking PR/Commit: https://github.com/k8snetworkplumbingwg/ptp-operator/pull/205 (SHA 84723c83c6, merged 2026-06-23, test code on main branch). The GetInterfaces bug pre-dates this PR; the PR exposed it by introducing the first multi-profile TBC PtpConfig. This is a main-only change; no cherry-pick to release-4.22 required (and none expected — test code always runs from main).
• Is it a flake? No
• Suggested Fix: Fix GetInterfaces in api/v1/ptpconfig_webhook.go to aggregate interfaces across all profiles (iterating config.Spec.Profile[:] instead of only Profile[0]), respecting the per-profile ptpRole field. Alternatively, add a separate GetInterfacesForTBC helper that is T-BC–aware and populate DiscoveredMasterInterfaces from the tbc-tt profile.

• Group 2: ptp4l log stream timeout + pmc bad-command (affects: "PTP daemon apply match rule based on nodeLabel", "Can provide a profile with higher priority")
• Evidence:

```

could not find regex in log stream for openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-l7mkn container=linuxptp-daemon-container, err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded

parentPortIdentity.clockIdentity not found in pmc output for /var/run/ptp4l.0.config: bad command: GET

bad command: PARENT_DATA_SET

GetClockIDForeign: log parsing failed for temp_temp, falling back to pmc: could not find regex in log stream...err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded

```

• Root Cause: Because masterIfCount: 0, ptp4l on the TBC node runs as a pure slave (no downstream master ports). In this state, (a) ptp4l does not emit the sync log lines the tests regex-match against, causing the 3-minute context deadline exceeded stream read timeout, and (b) when pmc issues GET PARENT_DATA_SET against the temp config's ptp4l socket at /var/run/ptp4l.0.config, ptp4l returns bad command: GET / bad command: PARENT_DATA_SET — likely because the T-BC ptp4l instance is using a separate UDS socket path (/var/run/ptp4l.tbc-tr.socket or similar) introduced by the dual-profile linuxptp-daemon configuration, while the test falls back to the default .0.config path. Both are downstream symptoms of the Group 1 discovery bug.
• Breaking PR/Commit: Same as Group 1 — PR #205 on main
• Is it a flake? No
• Suggested Fix: After fixing GetInterfaces, also verify that GetClockIDForeign uses the correct ptp4l socket path for multi-profile T-BC configs (the tbc-tr profile's socket, not the default ptp4l.0 path).

• Group 3: Downstream slave absent → ptpConfig nil (affects: "Downstream slave can sync to BC master")
• Evidence:

```

[FAILED] Expected

<*errors.fundamental | 0xc000ac2048>:

ptpConfig is nil

{msg: "ptpConfig is nil", ...}

to be nil

In [It] at: ptp.go:906

DiscoveredSlave1PtpConfig= nil

```

• Root Cause: The test at ptp.go:906 asserts that DiscoveredSlave1PtpConfig is non-nil, i.e., a downstream slave PtpConfig exists. Because GetInterfaces returns 0 master interfaces for the T-BC config, no slave lookup is performed and DiscoveredSlave1PtpConfig stays nil. The test fails immediately without any timing component.
• Breaking PR/Commit: Same as Group 1 — PR #205 on main
• Is it a flake? No
• Suggested Fix: Part of the Group 1 fix; once master interfaces are correctly discovered, the slave PtpConfig discovery should succeed provided the lab has a downstream slave node configured with a PtpConfig.

• Group 4: T-BC node unsynchronised — phc2sys down, clock class 165, OS FREERUN (affects: "Should restart phc2sys with no socket errors after SIGTERM", "Check GM announcements in locked mode and holdover transitions", "PTP Slave Clock Sync")
• Evidence:

```

[FAILED] Timed out after 180.001s.

Expected phc2sys process status to be 1 for GM

Expected : 0 to equal : 1


WARNING[ptp.go: 3170] Current Clock class: 165 (expected: 135) [repeats for 5 minutes]

Class 135 data not yet matching expected, retrying: AnnounceData mismatches:

• currentUtcOffsetValid: false vs true

[FAILED] Timed out after 300.000s. Clock should reach holdover (class 135) with matching data.


[FAILED] OS Clock was never LOCKED and test timed out

{"values":[...{"value":"FREERUN"},{"value":"-9"}]}

```

• Root Cause: The TBC node (cnfdf32, running linuxptp-daemon-l7mkn) is stuck in FREERUN state throughout all TBC tests: clock class stays at 165 (not-locked), phc2sys is not running (process status 0), and the OS clock never achieves LOCK. This is because the T-BC is not syncing at the PTP level — a direct consequence of the corrupted topology discovery from Group 1 (no master interfaces → the T-BC acts as a plain slave rather than a Boundary Clock, and phc2sys may not be started for a non-BC node). The holdover test additionally shows currentUtcOffsetValid: false, which means no valid UTC reference has been established. The 5+ minute stream of Clock class: 165 and the final transition to 248 (FREERUN/unsync) confirm the T-BC was never in a locked state during this test run.
• Breaking PR/Commit: Same as Group 1 — PR #205 on main
• Is it a flake? No
• Suggested Fix: Fix the Group 1 GetInterfaces bug. Additionally confirm the lab's T-BC node has valid GNSS/upstream PTP sync before running the holdover/outage tests, and ensure the test config deploys phc2sys in the tbc-tt (transmitter) profile with appropriate configuration.

Suite Issue Class: test_regression

Suite Severity: HIGH


tgm



Suite: tgm (2 failures)


Suite TL;DR: Both tgm-serial tests fail because the WPC T-GM hardware on cnfdf32.telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com does not reliably sustain holdover (clock class 7) after GNSS is disabled — one test sees class 7 never arrive within 3 minutes, and the other sees it flash through class 7 and immediately expire to class 248 (FREERUN).


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: WPC T-GM holdover (clock class 7) not reached or not sustained after GNSS disable (affects: both tests)
• Evidence:
• Test 1 (holdover through connection loss): After GNSS is disabled, waitForClockClass polls the Prometheus metrics endpoint every 2 s for up to 3 minutes. The log shows 80+ consecutive lines of Clock class not yet 7, retrying... followed by [FAILED] Timed out waiting for clock class 7 at ptp.go:4007 after 3 m 16 s. The metric openshift_ptp_clock_class{process="ptp4l"} never left 6.
• Test 2 (GNSS reboot and recovery): The cloud-event-proxy consumer received the following event sequence after GNSS disable: `PtpClockClassChange Event recieved map[metric:7], PtpClockClassChange Event recieved



TL;DR: TBC T-BC discovery is broken (6/9 failures); holdover flakiness and a phc2sys HA timeout account for the rest.


Root Cause Groups:


• 6/9 failures — T-BC discovery broken (GetInterfaces reads only Profile[0]): All 6 tbc suite tests fail because api/v1/ptpconfig_webhook.go::GetInterfaces unconditionally indexes config.Spec.Profile[0], leaving masterIfCount: 0 and DiscoveredMasterInterfaces=[] for the two-profile test-wpc-tbc PtpConfig. Introduced by ptp-operator PR #205 (merged 2026-06-23) exposing a pre-existing bug in the webhook.

• 2/9 failures — WPC T-GM holdover not reached/sustained (flaky hardware on cnfdf32): Both tgm-serial tests fail because the WPC T-GM on cnfdf32.telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com either never enters clock class 7 within 3 minutes, or flashes through class 7 so briefly the test cannot observe it before reverting to class 248 (FREERUN). Likely a near-zero LocalHoldoverTimeout or unreliable GNSS-disable simulation on this node's firmware.

• 1/9 failures — phc2sys HA failover timeout (dualnicbcha): phc2sys does not emit the secondary-interface selection log line within 60 s after ens2f1 is taken down; GetPodLogsRegexSince hits context deadline exceeded. Timing-sensitive; possibly a regression from linuxptp-daemon upstream sync commit fd6ff1e (2026-06-30), but unconfirmed.

Cross-Suite Patterns:


• Suite-specific: The tbc failure is entirely self-contained — a code bug in the webhook that no other suite triggers.
• Shared pattern (tbc Group 2 + dualnicbcha): Both tbc secondary failures and the dualnicbcha failure share the same surface symptom — context deadline exceeded from GetPodLogsRegexSince/log-stream polling — but for different underlying reasons (broken topology vs. slow daemon response). Not the same root cause.
• tgm is hardware-environment-specific: The holdover failures are isolated to one physical node (cnfdf32) and do not overlap with the software-side failures in the other suites.

Actionable Recommendations:


1. Fix GetInterfaces in api/v1/ptpconfig_webhook.go — iterate over all config.Spec.Profile[:] entries (not just [0]), aggregating master/slave interfaces by ptpRole. This is a one-line loop change that immediately unblocks all 6 TBC test failures (67% of the job).

2. Investigate cnfdf32 T-GM hardware configuration — check the LocalHoldoverTimeout value in the WPC HardwareConfig CRD on that node, and verify that ubxtool -z CFG-NAVSPG-INFIL_NCNOTHRS,50,1 reliably simulates GNSS loss on the installed firmware version. If the holdover window is hardware-limited, extend test polling budgets or skip on nodes known to have short holdover.

3. Add a passing-baseline check for the phc2sys HA failover test — instrument or log the time phc2sys takes to emit the secondary-interface selection after primary link-down, and compare against the 60 s timeout to determine whether the timeout needs extending or the linuxptp-daemon upstream sync introduced a regression in HA switching speed.

Overall Issue Class: test_regression (primary: code bug in webhook exposed by new TBC tests) + test_flake (secondary: hardware holdover and timing sensitivity)


Overall Severity: HIGH — 6 of 9 failures have a known, fixable root cause with a clear code change; the remaining 3 are environmental/timing-dependent.




Per-Suite Details



dualnicbcha



Suite: dualnicbcha (1 failures)


Suite TL;DR: phc2sys failed to emit a secondary-interface selection log line within the 60-second timeout after the primary BC slave interface (ens2f1) was deliberately taken down, causing the HA failover verification step to hit context deadline exceeded.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: phc2sys HA failover did not complete within test timeout (affects: [It] [dualnicbcha-serial] PTP e2e tests PTP ClockSync DualNICBCHA phc2sys switches to secondary ptp4l when primary interface fails [Serial])

• Evidence:

```

INFO [Jul 7 14:58:22.906][ptp.go: 1103] phc2sys log matching line: phc2sys[18333.639]: [phc2sys.2.config:6] selecting ens2f1 as out-of-domain source clock

INFO [Jul 7 14:58:25.557][ptptesthelper.go: 435] Turning interface: ens2f1 in pod ptp-network-outage-recovery-tl8kz down, stdout: , stderr:

STEP: Verifying phc2sys switches to a different interface - ptp.go:1125 @ 07/07/26 14:58:30.816

[FAILED] Unexpected error: could not find regex in log stream for openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-jz5vh container=linuxptp-daemon-container, err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded

In [It] at: /tmp/cnf-8b4Nw/ptp-operator-conformance-test/test/conformance/serial/ptp.go:1130 @ 07/07/26 14:59:30.817

```

Test ran for 73s total; the GetPodLogsRegexSince call watching for phc2sys(?m).?:. selecting (\w+) as out-of-domain source clock (at ptp.go:1125) exhausted pkg.TimeoutIn1Minute (60s) without observing the secondary interface being selected.


• Root Cause: phc2sys was correctly using ens2f1 as the out-of-domain clock source initially (HA discovery successful). After ens2f1 was forced down, phc2sys should switch to the secondary BC ptp4l socket (ens4f2) and emit a new selecting ... as out-of-domain source clock log entry. The switch did not produce that log line within 60 seconds. The delay is timing-sensitive: it depends on how long ptp4l on the primary socket takes to enter HOLDOVER/FREERUN and how quickly phc2sys's HA source selection responds. The test assertion at ptp.go:1130 calls Expect(err).NotTo(HaveOccurred()) on the return of pods.GetPodLogsRegexSince — when the stream context deadline expires before any regex match, err is non-nil and the test fails. Note: the WARNING ptp4l conf parsing failed, err=config option not in section for test-dual-nic-bc-ha is expected (the HA profile has no standalone ptp4l config body) and does not affect runtime behavior.

• Breaking PR/Commit: Possibly linuxptp-daemon PR #617 (fd6ff1e, merged 2026-06-30, "CNF-22056, CNF-22693, CNF-25167, OCPBUGS-85586, OCPBUGS-93744: Sync from upstream (30-Jun-2026)") — this was a large initial upstream sync (added pkg/daemon/daemon.go, log_parsing.go, and the full daemon codebase in a single commit one week before this run). However, without a history of prior passing runs to compare against, this cannot be confirmed as the causal change. Breaking PR/Commit: Unknown (possible fd6ff1e in k8snetworkplumbingwg/linuxptp-daemon).

• Is it a flake? Yes. The HA switch itself was architecturally working (phc2sys correctly selected the primary interface on startup). The failure is a timing race between the hardware/kernel reacting to the link-down event, ptp4l entering HOLDOVER, phc2sys detecting the source degradation, and the 60-second test deadline. This test has historically been susceptible to this pattern on real hardware.

• Suggested Fix: Retry the job to confirm flakiness. If it recurs, increase pkg.TimeoutIn1Minute for the GetPodLogsRegexSince call at ptp.go:1125 to a longer window (e.g., 2–3 minutes), or investigate whether PR #617 changed phc2sys HA source-selection timing (e.g., holdover duration or polling interval) in the k8snetworkplumbingwg/linuxptp-daemon main branch.

Suite Issue Class: test_flake

Suite Severity: LOW


tbc



Suite: tbc (6 failures)


Suite TL;DR: All 6 TBC tests fail because the test-wpc-tbc PtpConfig is discovered with masterIfCount: 0 and no downstream slave PtpConfig, caused by a bug in api/v1/ptpconfig_webhook.go::GetInterfaces that only reads Profile[0] of a multi-profile T-BC config — leaving the TBC unable to sync and the test topology incomplete.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: T-BC discovery broken — GetInterfaces reads only Profile[0] (affects: all 6 tests)
• Evidence:

```

WARNING[Jul 7 13:24:41.891][ptpconfig_webhook.go: 457] More than one profile detected for ptpconfig test-wpc-tbc

INFO[testconfig.go: 2172] ptptConfig: test-wpc-tbc, masterIfCount: 0, slaveIfCount: 1

INFO[testconfig.go: 2207] Detected T-BC configuration with tbc-tr and tbc-tt profiles

INFO[testconfig.go: 940] ...DiscoveredSlave1PtpConfig= nil, DiscoveredMasterInterfaces=[]DiscoveredFollowerInterfaces=[ens7f0 ens7f0]...

```

• Root Cause: api/v1/ptpconfig_webhook.go line 455, function GetInterfaces, unconditionally uses config.Spec.Profile[0] even when a PtpConfig has multiple profiles. The T-BC config test-wpc-tbc has two profiles: tbc-tr (upstream slave) at Profile[0] and tbc-tt (downstream master) at Profile[1]. Because GetInterfaces ignores Profile[1], masterIfCount is always 0 for any T-BC config. The warning More than one profile detected fires at line 457 but the function proceeds anyway. This leaves DiscoveredMasterInterfaces=[] and DiscoveredSlave1PtpConfig=nil throughout all TBC tests. T-BC tests were introduced in ptp-operator PR #205 merged 2026-06-23 on main — two weeks before this job ran — and appear never to have passed end-to-end due to this bug.
• Breaking PR/Commit: https://github.com/k8snetworkplumbingwg/ptp-operator/pull/205 (SHA 84723c83c6, merged 2026-06-23, test code on main branch). The GetInterfaces bug pre-dates this PR; the PR exposed it by introducing the first multi-profile TBC PtpConfig. This is a main-only change; no cherry-pick to release-4.22 required (and none expected — test code always runs from main).
• Is it a flake? No
• Suggested Fix: Fix GetInterfaces in api/v1/ptpconfig_webhook.go to aggregate interfaces across all profiles (iterating config.Spec.Profile[:] instead of only Profile[0]), respecting the per-profile ptpRole field. Alternatively, add a separate GetInterfacesForTBC helper that is T-BC–aware and populate DiscoveredMasterInterfaces from the tbc-tt profile.

• Group 2: ptp4l log stream timeout + pmc bad-command (affects: "PTP daemon apply match rule based on nodeLabel", "Can provide a profile with higher priority")
• Evidence:

```

could not find regex in log stream for openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-l7mkn container=linuxptp-daemon-container, err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded

parentPortIdentity.clockIdentity not found in pmc output for /var/run/ptp4l.0.config: bad command: GET

bad command: PARENT_DATA_SET

GetClockIDForeign: log parsing failed for temp_temp, falling back to pmc: could not find regex in log stream...err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded

```

• Root Cause: Because masterIfCount: 0, ptp4l on the TBC node runs as a pure slave (no downstream master ports). In this state, (a) ptp4l does not emit the sync log lines the tests regex-match against, causing the 3-minute context deadline exceeded stream read timeout, and (b) when pmc issues GET PARENT_DATA_SET against the temp config's ptp4l socket at /var/run/ptp4l.0.config, ptp4l returns bad command: GET / bad command: PARENT_DATA_SET — likely because the T-BC ptp4l instance is using a separate UDS socket path (/var/run/ptp4l.tbc-tr.socket or similar) introduced by the dual-profile linuxptp-daemon configuration, while the test falls back to the default .0.config path. Both are downstream symptoms of the Group 1 discovery bug.
• Breaking PR/Commit: Same as Group 1 — PR #205 on main
• Is it a flake? No
• Suggested Fix: After fixing GetInterfaces, also verify that GetClockIDForeign uses the correct ptp4l socket path for multi-profile T-BC configs (the tbc-tr profile's socket, not the default ptp4l.0 path).

• Group 3: Downstream slave absent → ptpConfig nil (affects: "Downstream slave can sync to BC master")
• Evidence:

```

[FAILED] Expected

<*errors.fundamental | 0xc000ac2048>:

ptpConfig is nil

{msg: "ptpConfig is nil", ...}

to be nil

In [It] at: ptp.go:906

DiscoveredSlave1PtpConfig= nil

```

• Root Cause: The test at ptp.go:906 asserts that DiscoveredSlave1PtpConfig is non-nil, i.e., a downstream slave PtpConfig exists. Because GetInterfaces returns 0 master interfaces for the T-BC config, no slave lookup is performed and DiscoveredSlave1PtpConfig stays nil. The test fails immediately without any timing component.
• Breaking PR/Commit: Same as Group 1 — PR #205 on main
• Is it a flake? No
• Suggested Fix: Part of the Group 1 fix; once master interfaces are correctly discovered, the slave PtpConfig discovery should succeed provided the lab has a downstream slave node configured with a PtpConfig.

• Group 4: T-BC node unsynchronised — phc2sys down, clock class 165, OS FREERUN (affects: "Should restart phc2sys with no socket errors after SIGTERM", "Check GM announcements in locked mode and holdover transitions", "PTP Slave Clock Sync")
• Evidence:

```

[FAILED] Timed out after 180.001s.

Expected phc2sys process status to be 1 for GM

Expected : 0 to equal : 1


WARNING[ptp.go: 3170] Current Clock class: 165 (expected: 135) [repeats for 5 minutes]

Class 135 data not yet matching expected, retrying: AnnounceData mismatches:

• currentUtcOffsetValid: false vs true

[FAILED] Timed out after 300.000s. Clock should reach holdover (class 135) with matching data.


[FAILED] OS Clock was never LOCKED and test timed out

{"values":[...{"value":"FREERUN"},{"value":"-9"}]}

```

• Root Cause: The TBC node (cnfdf32, running linuxptp-daemon-l7mkn) is stuck in FREERUN state throughout all TBC tests: clock class stays at 165 (not-locked), phc2sys is not running (process status 0), and the OS clock never achieves LOCK. This is because the T-BC is not syncing at the PTP level — a direct consequence of the corrupted topology discovery from Group 1 (no master interfaces → the T-BC acts as a plain slave rather than a Boundary Clock, and phc2sys may not be started for a non-BC node). The holdover test additionally shows currentUtcOffsetValid: false, which means no valid UTC reference has been established. The 5+ minute stream of Clock class: 165 and the final transition to 248 (FREERUN/unsync) confirm the T-BC was never in a locked state during this test run.
• Breaking PR/Commit: Same as Group 1 — PR #205 on main
• Is it a flake? No
• Suggested Fix: Fix the Group 1 GetInterfaces bug. Additionally confirm the lab's T-BC node has valid GNSS/upstream PTP sync before running the holdover/outage tests, and ensure the test config deploys phc2sys in the tbc-tt (transmitter) profile with appropriate configuration.

Suite Issue Class: test_regression

Suite Severity: HIGH


tgm



Suite: tgm (2 failures)


Suite TL;DR: Both tgm-serial tests fail because the WPC T-GM hardware on cnfdf32.telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com does not reliably sustain holdover (clock class 7) after GNSS is disabled — one test sees class 7 never arrive within 3 minutes, and the other sees it flash through class 7 and immediately expire to class 248 (FREERUN).


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: WPC T-GM holdover (clock class 7) not reached or not sustained after GNSS disable (affects: both tests)
• Evidence:
• Test 1 (holdover through connection loss): After GNSS is disabled, waitForClockClass polls the Prometheus metrics endpoint every 2 s for up to 3 minutes. The log shows 80+ consecutive lines of Clock class not yet 7, retrying... followed by [FAILED] Timed out waiting for clock class 7 at ptp.go:4007 after 3 m 16 s. The metric openshift_ptp_clock_class{process="ptp4l"} never left 6.
• Test 2 (GNSS reboot and recovery): The cloud-event-proxy consumer received the following event sequence after GNSS disable: `PtpClockClassChange Event recieved map[metric:7], PtpClockClassChange Event recieved
2026-07-07T12:30
❌ failure4.234.23-e2e-telco5g-ptp276mtest_flake LOW
WPC T-GM node cnfdf32 DPLL never entered HOLDOVER (clock class 7) after GNSS coldboot.
3 failed tests
INFRA LOW
🆕 New issue
Full AI Analysis

TL;DR: Two test-code regressions and one GNSS hardware flake caused all 5 failures across bc, dualnicbcha, and tgm suites.



Root Cause Groups:


• 3/5 failures — GNSS hardware/signal flake on telco5g lab nodes (tgm suite: Verify ts2phc metrics cleaned up, Testing WPC T-GM holdover through connection loss, Testing T-GM Events on GNSS reboot and recovery): All 3 tgm tests returned Ginkgo error (panic/timeout before assertions), blanket-failing every GNSS-dependent test in the tgm suite while all other suites skipped those tests cleanly. No code regression is evidenced; the nightly image resolved and imported cleanly.

• 1/5 failures — Nil DiscoveredSlave1PtpConfig race condition in test setup (bc suite: Downstream slave can sync to BC master): Test completed in ~4 seconds via the immediate-return ptpConfig is nil path in BasicClockSyncCheck. The BC-with-slaves topology was detected, but the slave PtpConfig reference was not captured before the test ran — a TOCTOU race between topology discovery and PtpConfig API enumeration that commit 3b93c9f left unguarded.

• 1/5 failures — bb43e35 phc2sys timing adaptation incompatible with 1-minute log window (dualnicbcha suite: DualNICBCHA phc2sys switches to secondary ptp4l when primary interface fails): Commit bb43e35 (Jun 23, 2026) added a 5-minute wait for ptp4l SLAVE state before immediately searching phc2sys logs with a 1-minute window. If linuxptp-daemon#215 is present in the release-4.22 image (delaying phc2sys startup until ptp4l syncs), the 1-minute log-search window may expire before phc2sys emits its interface-selection log.


Cross-Suite Patterns:


• tgm-specific: All 3 GNSS hardware failures are isolated to the tgm suite, which is the only suite running tests against real WPC T-GM hardware (cnfdf30–cnfdf32). No other suite is affected.
• bc-specific: The nil-PtpConfig race is bc-serial-only; dualnicbcha and tgm do not exercise the DiscoveredSlave1PtpConfig discovery path.
• dualnicbcha-specific: The phc2sys HA timing failure is isolated to the dualnicbcha suite and is a direct consequence of a recent main-branch test change not fully validated against the release-4.22 production image.
• Common thread (bc + dualnicbcha): Both non-GNSS failures stem from the TEST_BRANCH=main / PTP_UNDER_TEST_BRANCH=release-4.22 split — test code on main is ahead of or not fully compatible with the release-4.22 operator image, producing timing/discovery failures that would not appear if both branches matched.


Actionable Recommendations:


1. Fix the DiscoveredSlave1PtpConfig nil guard (bc suite): In test/pkg/ptptesthelper/ptptesthelper.go, wrap the DiscoveredSlave1PtpConfig assignment site in an Eventually retry (analogous to the 3b93c9f fix for clock ID lookups) so the test retries discovery if the slave PtpConfig is not yet reflected in the API server.

2. Extend or retry the phc2sys log-search window (dualnicbcha suite): In ptp.go around line 1098, replace the single GetPodLogsRegex call with an Eventually loop (e.g., 5 minutes, 10-second poll) consistent with how the preceding ptp4l SLAVE wait is structured, so the test tolerates variable delay between ptp4l synchronization and phc2sys log emission.

3. Retry the tgm job to confirm transience: The 3 GNSS failures show no code regression. Re-run the periodic-ci-openshift-release-main-nightly-4.22-e2e-telco5g-ptp job; if tgm passes on retry, file a lab-infra ticket tracking the GNSS signal reliability on cnfdf30–cnfdf32.


Overall Issue Class: test_flake (tgm: infra/hardware flake) + test_regression (bc and dualnicbcha: main test-code issues)

Overall Severity: MEDIUM




Per-Suite Details



bc



Suite: bc (1 failures)


Suite TL;DR: The "Downstream slave can sync to BC master" test failed in ~4 seconds due to a nil DiscoveredSlave1PtpConfig reference, indicating a race condition in test-setup discovery that caused BasicClockSyncCheck to return immediately with "ptpConfig is nil."


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: Nil DiscoveredSlave1PtpConfig race condition in BC test (affects: [It] [bc-serial] PTP e2e tests PTP ClockSync Downstream slave can sync to BC master [Serial])
• Evidence:
• From telco5g-ptp-tests__test_results.json: "[It] [bc-serial] PTP e2e tests PTP ClockSync Downstream slave can sync to BC master [Serial]": {"time": 4.097216612, "result": "error"}
• The JUnit XML header confirms this is a JUnit element (), mapped to "error" by the telco5gci junit2json.py script — meaning an assertion failure, not a panic.
• Test ran in only 4.097 seconds. Every retry path in the test (Eventually(..., pkg.TimeoutIn3Minutes, pkg.Timeout10Seconds)) would take 3+ minutes to time out. A 4-second failure is only possible via an immediate-return error path.
• From ci-operator-build-log.txt: TEST_BRANCH=main, PTP_UNDER_TEST_BRANCH=release-4.22. The Prow step (telco5g-ptp-tests) clones https://github.com/k8snetworkplumbingwg/ptp-operator.git -b main as ptp-operator-conformance-test for the test scripts, and deploys openshift/ptp-operator:release-4.22 images.
• Root Cause: The test code in k8snetworkplumbingwg/ptp-operator:main at test/conformance/serial/ptp.go:905 calls ptptesthelper.BasicClockSyncCheck(fullConfig, (*ptpv1.PtpConfig)(fullConfig.DiscoveredSlave1PtpConfig), ...). Inside BasicClockSyncCheck (test/pkg/ptptesthelper/ptptesthelper.go:36), the first check is if ptpConfig == nil { return errors.Errorf("ptpConfig is nil") }, which returns immediately. DiscoveredSlave1PtpConfig is populated in testconfig.go:2111 only when a PTP config with node label ptp/test-slave1 is found during the discovery pass; if the discovery race fires before the slave PTP config is reflected in the API server, it remains nil. The test passed the skip guard (FoundSolutions[AlgoBCWithSlavesString] == true), meaning the L2 topology solver detected a BC-with-slaves network, but the slave PTP config reference itself was not captured — a classic TOCTOU race between topology discovery and PtpConfig list enumeration. Commit 3b93c9f ("tests: add retries to PTP clock ID lookups") added Eventually retries for GetClockIDMaster to handle exactly this kind of reconciliation race, but left the DiscoveredSlave1PtpConfig nil-dereference site unguarded.
• Breaking PR/Commit: The immediate trigger is a race condition in test setup, not a single commit. Closest relevant commit: 3b93c9f (k8snetworkplumbingwg/ptp-operator) — added Eventually retries for clock ID lookups but did not add a corresponding nil guard or retry for DiscoveredSlave1PtpConfig. The check_backport.py script referenced in the prompt was not present in the repo (scripts/check_backport.py not found); manual inspection of openshift/ptp-operator:release-4.22 branch shows 3b93c9f is NOT present there, but it applies to the test scripts (k8snetworkplumbingwg), not the images.
• Is it a flake? Yes. The identical test in the dualnicbc suite passed in the same job run ("[It] [dualnicbc-serial] PTP e2e tests PTP ClockSync Downstream slave can sync to BC master [Serial]": {"time": 13.593677095, "result": "pass"}), confirming the test logic is valid when DiscoveredSlave1PtpConfig is correctly populated. The investigation-summary.txt also classifies this as Severity: LOW / infra transient.
• Suggested Fix: Add a Expect(fullConfig.DiscoveredSlave1PtpConfig).ToNot(BeNil(), "slave1 PtpConfig must be discovered before BC sync check") guard immediately before the BasicClockSyncCheck call at test/conformance/serial/ptp.go:905, or wrap the discovery + check in an Eventually block (similar to the GetClockIDMaster retry added in 3b93c9f) to tolerate slow operator reconciliation in the bc set



TL;DR: WPC T-GM node cnfdf32 DPLL never entered HOLDOVER (clock class 7) after GNSS coldboot.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: DPLL fails to reach/maintain clock class 7 (HOLDOVER) after GNSS loss (affects Tests: both)
• Evidence:

```

Test 1 — DPLL stuck at FREERUN for full 200s after GNSS disabled:

Matched line: [ptp4l 248] → GNSS disabled → Clock class not yet 7, retrying... (×32+)

→ GNSS enabled → [FAILED] Timed out waiting for clock class 7 (ptp.go:3802, 3m20s elapsed)


Test 2 — class 7 transient, immediately overtaken by 248:

PtpClockClassChange Event recieved map[metric:6], map[metric:7], map[metric:248]

GnssStateChange Event recieved map[metric:0 notification:FAILURE-NOFIX]

→ STEP: Verifying ClockClass value to 7 → [FAILED] expected a metric [7.000000] but got map[metric:248] (ptp.go:3898)

```

• Root Cause: After GNSS signal loss on node cnfdf32.telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com, the WPC hardware DPLL transitions directly from LOCKED to FREERUN (class 248) without sustaining HOLDOVER (class 7). In pkg/event/event.go, clock class 7 requires dpllState == PTP_HOLDOVER; since the DPLL reports FREERUN immediately, that path is never taken. In test 2, HOLDOVER was briefly entered (class 7 visible in the event stream) but exhausted faster than the metric-polling step could verify it — confirming the DPLL holdover window on this hardware is abnormally short.
• Breaking PR/Commit: Unknown — the upstream sync PR #613 (0459bc0, CNF-24996/OCPBUGS-90708, merged 2026-06-23) was an upstream sync to the main branch and does not directly affect the release-4.22 branch under test; no code regression identified.
• Is it a flake? Yes — test 2 shows holdover does occur but transiently; this is a hardware-timing sensitivity, consistent with intermittent failures when DPLL holdover duration is at the margins.
• Suggested Fix: Investigate DPLL holdover configuration on cnfdf32 (check LocalHoldoverTimeout configured for the WPC hardware); if hardware is degraded or misconfigured for holdover, reprovisioning or reconfiguring the node should resolve it. Retry the job to confirm transience.
• Affected tests:
• [It] [tgm-serial] PTP e2e tests WPC GM GNSS signal loss tests Testing WPC T-GM holdover through connection loss [Serial]
• [It] [tgm-serial] PTP e2e tests WPC GM Events verification (V2) Testing T-GM Events on GNSS reboot and recovery [Serial]


Relation Between Groups: Single group — both failures share the same root cause: DPLL on cnfdf32 not sustaining HOLDOVER state long enough after GNSS loss. Test 1 never sees class 7 at all; test 2 sees it transiently. Both are direct manifestations of the same abnormally short holdover window on this hardware.


Per-Version Notes: OCP 4.22 (nightly-2026-06-23-164229) — both failures in the [tgm-serial] suite only; all other suites (bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc) show 0 failures and passed cleanly.


Affected Images: image-registry.openshift-image-registry.svc:5000/openshift-ptp/ptp-operator:4.22, image-registry.openshift-image-registry.svc:5000/openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon:4.22, image-registry.openshift-image-registry.svc:5000/openshift-ptp/cloud-event-proxy:4.22


Overall Issue Class: test_flake


Overall Severity: LOW

Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: DPLL fails to reach/maintain clock class 7 (HOLDOVER) after GNSS loss (affects Tests: both)

```

Test 1 — DPLL stuck at FREERUN for full 200s after GNSS disabled:

Matched line: [ptp4l 248] → GNSS disabled → Clock class not yet 7, retrying... (×32+)

→ GNSS enabled → [FAILED] Timed out waiting for clock class 7 (ptp.go:3802, 3m20s elapsed)

→ STEP: Verifying ClockClass value to 7 → [FAILED] expected a metric [7.000000] but got map[metric:248] (ptp.go:3898)

In pkg/event/event.go, clock class 7 requires dpllState == PTP_HOLDOVER; since the DPLL reports FREERUN immediately, that path is never taken. In test 2, HOLDOVER was briefly entered (class 7 visible in the event stream) but exhausted faster than the metric-polling step could verify it — confirming the DPLL holdover window on this hardware is abnormally short.
• Breaking PR/Commit: Unknown — the upstream sync PR #613 (0459bc0, CNF-24996/OCPBUGS-90708, merged 2026-06-23) was an upstream sync to the main branch and does not directly affect the release-4.22 branch under test; no code regression identified.
• Is it a flake? Yes — test 2 shows holdover does occur but transiently; this is a hardware-timing sensitivity, consistent with intermittent failures when DPLL holdover duration is at the margins.
• Suggested Fix: Investigate DPLL holdover configuration on cnfdf32 (check LocalHoldoverTimeout configured for the WPC hardware); if hardware is degraded or misconfigured for holdover, reprovisioning or reconfiguring the node should resolve it. Retry the job to confirm transience.

• [It] [tgm-serial] PTP e2e tests WPC GM GNSS signal loss tests Testing WPC T-GM holdover through connection loss [Serial]
• [It] [tgm-serial] PTP e2e tests WPC GM Events verification (V2) Testing T-GM Events on GNSS reboot and recovery [Serial]


Relation Between Groups: Single group — both failures share the same root cause: DPLL on cnfdf32 not sustaining HOLDOVER state long enough after GNSS loss. Test 1 never sees class 7 at all; test 2 sees it transiently. Both are direct manifestations of the same abnormally short holdover window on this hardware.


Per-Version Notes: OCP 4.22 (nightly-2026-06-23-164229) — both failures in the [tgm-serial] suite only; all other suites (bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc) show 0 failures and passed cleanly.


Affected Images: image-registry.openshift-image-registry.svc:5000/openshift-ptp/ptp-operator:4.22, image-registry.openshift-image-registry.svc:5000/openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon:4.22, image-registry.openshift-image-registry.svc:5000/openshift-ptp/cloud-event-proxy:4.22


Overall Issue Class: test_flake


Overall Severity: LOW

2026-07-05T00:01
⚠️ Test Failures
❌ failure4.234.23-e2e-telco5g-ptp-upstream60minfra_other MEDIUM
CI build cluster had zero schedulable nodes for the release-import pod; job never reached PTP tests.
1 failed test
TEST MEDIUM
🆕 New issue
Full AI Analysis

TL;DR: CI build cluster had zero schedulable nodes for the release-import pod; job never reached PTP tests.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: CI build-cluster node scheduling exhaustion (affects Tests: "Import the release payload 'latest' from an external source")
• Evidence:

```

INFO[2026-07-05T06:01:01Z] pod pending for more than 1h0m0s: pod has not been scheduled in 1h0m0.000454498s

0/57 nodes are available: 1 node(s) were unschedulable, 11 node(s) didn't match Pod's node affinity/selector, 45 node(s) had untolerated taint(s).

INFO[2026-07-05T06:01:01Z] Reporting job state 'failed' with reason 'executing_graph:step_failed:importing_release:pod_pending'

```

• Root Cause: The CI operator created pod release-images-latest-cli on build cluster build10 at 05:01:01Z to import registry.ci.openshift.org/ocp/release:4.23.0-0.nightly-2026-07-04-165958. For the entire 1-hour window, 0 of 52–57 nodes were schedulable: 11 consistently failed node affinity/selector, and the rest carried untolerated taints. The multiarch-tuning-operator did process and gate-remove the pod (set amd64 arch), but the scheduler still found no viable node. The PTP e2e test step was never reached.
• Breaking PR/Commit: Unknown — this is a CI build-cluster capacity/taint issue, not a code change.
• Is it a flake? Yes — node exhaustion/taint saturation on the shared build cluster is transient and unrelated to PTP operator code.
• Suggested Fix: Retry the job during off-peak hours; if it recurs, report to DPTP (OpenShift CI infra team) that build10 is saturated with untolerated taints blocking release-import pods.
• Affected tests: "Import the release payload 'latest' from an external source"


Relation Between Groups: Single group; one root cause cascaded to abort the entire job before any PTP test ran.


Per-Version Notes: OCP 4.23 — nightly 4.23.0-0.nightly-2026-07-04-165958 import blocked; no PTP test results for this run.


Affected Images: registry.ci.openshift.org/ocp/release:4.23.0-0.nightly-2026-07-04-165958 (specifically the cli component extracted from it via pod release-images-latest-cli)


Overall Issue Class: infra_other


Overall Severity: MEDIUM

2026-07-05T05:00
❌ failure5.05.0-e2e-telco5g-ptp392mtest_flake` (dominant: GNSS hardware failure is a known lab environmental flake) + `test_regression` (secondary: pmc argument bug introduced with RHCOS 10.2 / linuxptp upstream sync `0459bc0`) CRITICAL
WPC T-GM grandmaster never achieved GNSS lock (class 248), collapsing all 4 suites; pmc argument bug added 5 independent failures.
32 failed tests
TEST MEDIUM
🆕 New issue
Full AI Analysis

TL;DR: WPC T-GM grandmaster never achieved GNSS lock (class 248), collapsing all 4 suites; pmc argument bug added 5 independent failures.



Root Cause Groups:


• 20/25 failures — GM in FREERUN (clock class 248), cascading to all BC/slave/follower sync tests

Affects: tgm (5/5), dualnicbcha (~6/8), dualnicbc (~6/9), dualfollower (3/3). The WPC T-GM node hosting linuxptp-daemon-7g7z8 never transitioned out of FREERUN during the run. With no locked GM advertising Announce messages, the entire downstream chain — BC → dualnicbc → dualnicbcha → dualfollower slaves — could not lock. The tgm log stream stayed open but hit context deadline exceeded after 340 s without ever emitting a LOCKED event; all four state-based tgm tests timed out on Expected ptp4l clock class to eventually be 6 for GM / : false. The dualfollower node cnfdf31 exhibits both missing openshift_ptp_clock_state metric for ens2f0np0 and ens2f3np3 stuck in LISTENING — consistent with no upstream Announce to trigger SLAVE election.


• 5/25 failures — pmc invoked with split arguments; GET PARENT_DATA_SET rejected as two bad commands

Affects: dualnicbc ("Slave can sync to master", "Downstream slave can sync to BC master", "Can provide a profile with higher priority") and dualnicbcha ("Slave can sync to master", "Downstream slave can sync to BC master"). pmc is called with GET and PARENT_DATA_SET as separate argv elements; the binary responds bad command: GET / bad command: PARENT_DATA_SET. Identical verbatim error appears in both suites, ruling out a flake. Likely triggered by the RHCOS 10.2 pmc binary (linuxptp ≥ 4.3, el10_2) changing batch-mode argument tokenization, or the test helper failing to quote the combined string. Other pmc queries (e.g. GET DEFAULT_DATA_SET) still succeed, confirming the socket and ptp4l process are reachable.



Cross-Suite Patterns:


Root Causedualfollowerdualnicbcdualnicbchatgm
GM FREERUN / GNSS never lockedAll 3~6~6All 5
pmc split-arg bug—32—

• The GM FREERUN failure is shared across all 4 suites — it is the single largest driver and the only failure in tgm and dualfollower.
• The pmc split-arg bug is shared between dualnicbc and dualnicbcha only (suites that exercise PARENT_DATA_SET queries via the BC node).
• No failures are genuinely suite-specific; the dualfollower and tgm failures are pure downstream consequences of the shared GM not locking.


Actionable Recommendations:


1. Re-run the job and inspect GM GNSS hardware first. Confirm whether the WPC T-GM node achieves GNSS/DPLL lock in a fresh run. If it fails again, check the GNSS antenna cable, receiver status, and ts2phc DPLL netlink events on that node. This single fix would resolve ~20/25 failures.

2. Fix pmc invocation to pass GET PARENT_DATA_SET as a single quoted argument. In both the test helper (ptp-operator-conformance-test) and any exec.Command / shell call in linuxptp-daemon, ensure the action+management-ID are passed as one argument string (e.g. exec.Command("pmc", ..., "GET PARENT_DATA_SET")), or switch to the interactive RunPMCExpGetParentDS path with lowercase command ("get PARENT_DATA_SET") to match the RHCOS 10.2 pmc binary's expected input. This resolves the remaining 5 failures.

3. Add a pre-flight GM-lock gate to the CI job. Before running any BC/slave/follower suite, assert that the T-GM node has clock class ≤ 7 (LOCKED); fail fast with a clear infra error rather than letting all 4 suites time out. This would surface the GNSS issue immediately and save ~30 min of wasted CI time per run.


Overall Issue Class: test_flake (dominant: GNSS hardware failure is a known lab environmental flake) + test_regression (secondary: pmc argument bug introduced with RHCOS 10.2 / linuxptp upstream sync 0459bc0)


Overall Severity: CRITICAL — 25/25 failures, 4/4 suites completely down in a nightly periodic job.




Per-Suite Details



dualfollower



Suite: dualfollower (3 failures)


Suite TL;DR: All 3 dualfollower ClockSync tests timed out waiting for node cnfdf31's follower interfaces to reach LOCKED state because the openshift_ptp_clock_state metric for ens2f0np0 was never emitted by linuxptp-daemon, and ens2f3np3 was stuck in LISTENING rather than SLAVE role.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: Slave node never locked — missing clock_state metric and port stuck in LISTENING (affects: all 3 tests)
• Evidence:

```

Tests 1 & 2 — identical error

no Follower port in expected LOCKED state for ptpconfig test-slave1

(node=cnfdf31.telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com, label="ptp/clock-under-test",

slaveIfs=[ens2f0np0 ens2f3np3]). Per-interface results:

ens2f0np0: clock state metric error: metric: openshift_ptp_clock_state,

nodeName: cnfdf31.telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com, aIf: ens2f0np0 not found,

available op[tions: ...]

```

```

Test 3 — additional symptom

no Follower port in expected LOCKED state for ptpconfig temp

(node=cnfdf31.telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com, label="",

slaveIfs=[ens2f3np3 ens2f0np0]). Per-interface results:

ens2f3np3: role mismatch (expected=SLAVE, got=LISTENING)

ens2f0np0: clock state metric error: metric: openshift_ptp_clock_state,

nodeName: cnfdf31.telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com, aIf: ens2f0np0 not found

```

```

Broader context from other suites (junit-failures.txt)

parentPortIdentity.clockIdentity not found in pmc output for /var/run/ptp4l.1.config:

bad command: GET

bad command: PARENT_DATA_SET

```

```

tgm suite (junit-failures.txt)

Expected ptp4l clock class state to eventually be Locked (class 6)

...clock class: 248 (← default/FREERUN value, never reached 6)

```

• Root Cause: The openshift_ptp_clock_state metric (registered in linuxptp-daemon/pkg/metrics/register.go:57-63 with labels {process, node, iface}) is only set when UpdateClockStateMetrics() in pkg/metrics/exporter.go:15-23 is called with a servo state change for that interface. A metric label combination {node=cnfdf31, iface=ens2f0np0} being entirely absent means linuxptp-daemon on cnfdf31 never processed a servo state log line for ens2f0np0 — i.e., ptp4l never elected that port as SLAVE and never emitted a servo lock event. The explicit role mismatch (expected=SLAVE, got=LISTENING) in Test 3 for ens2f3np3 corroborates this: both follower ports are stuck in LISTENING/unelected state. The tgm suite failures (clock class 248 = freerun, never 6) indicate the upstream Grandmaster never achieved GNSS lock; with the T-GM in freerun the entire downstream chain — BC → dualfollower slave — cannot lock, which is consistent with all suites failing in the same run. Additionally, the pmc bad command: GET / PARENT_DATA_SET errors in the dualnicbc suite (same run, linuxptp-daemon branch release-5.0) indicate a ptp4l management interface issue. The commit OCPBUGS-90708: Sync from upstream (22-Jun-2026) in linuxptp-daemon (merged 2026-06-23, two days after this run) and the commit OCPBUGS-88369: Fix CLOCK_REALTIME dual-publisher conflict in ParseTBCLogs in cloud-event-proxy (merged 2026-06-25) were both pending at the time of this job.
• Breaking PR/Commit: Likely pre-dates the fix in linuxptp-daemon PR #613 (OCPBUGS-90708 / CNF-24996, merged 2026-06-23, SHA 0459bc0) and cloud-event-proxy PR #701 (OCPBUGS-88369, merged 2026-06-25, SHA fc5d567) — both merged after this CI run on 2026-06-21. The breaking change introducing the regression is Unknown (upstream/downstream commit not visible in the shallow clone).
• Is it a flake? No — all four test suites in the same job (dualfollower, dualnicbc, dualnicbcha, tgm) failed with consistent, correlated errors. The T-GM staying at clock class 248 is a systematic fa


TL;DR: WPC T-GM grandmaster never achieved GNSS lock (class 248), collapsing all 4 suites; pmc argument bug added 5 independent failures.



Root Cause Groups:


• 20/25 failures — GM in FREERUN (clock class 248), cascading to all BC/slave/follower sync tests

Affects: tgm (5/5), dualnicbcha (~6/8), dualnicbc (~6/9), dualfollower (3/3). The WPC T-GM node hosting linuxptp-daemon-7g7z8 never transitioned out of FREERUN during the run. With no locked GM advertising Announce messages, the entire downstream chain — BC → dualnicbc → dualnicbcha → dualfollower slaves — could not lock. The tgm log stream stayed open but hit context deadline exceeded after 340 s without ever emitting a LOCKED event; all four state-based tgm tests timed out on Expected ptp4l clock class to eventually be 6 for GM / : false. The dualfollower node cnfdf31 exhibits both missing openshift_ptp_clock_state metric for ens2f0np0 and ens2f3np3 stuck in LISTENING — consistent with no upstream Announce to trigger SLAVE election.


• 5/25 failures — pmc invoked with split arguments; GET PARENT_DATA_SET rejected as two bad commands

Affects: dualnicbc ("Slave can sync to master", "Downstream slave can sync to BC master", "Can provide a profile with higher priority") and dualnicbcha ("Slave can sync to master", "Downstream slave can sync to BC master"). pmc is called with GET and PARENT_DATA_SET as separate argv elements; the binary responds bad command: GET / bad command: PARENT_DATA_SET. Identical verbatim error appears in both suites, ruling out a flake. Likely triggered by the RHCOS 10.2 pmc binary (linuxptp ≥ 4.3, el10_2) changing batch-mode argument tokenization, or the test helper failing to quote the combined string. Other pmc queries (e.g. GET DEFAULT_DATA_SET) still succeed, confirming the socket and ptp4l process are reachable.



Cross-Suite Patterns:


Root Causedualfollowerdualnicbcdualnicbchatgm
GM FREERUN / GNSS never lockedAll 3~6~6All 5
pmc split-arg bug—32—

• The GM FREERUN failure is shared across all 4 suites — it is the single largest driver and the only failure in tgm and dualfollower.
• The pmc split-arg bug is shared between dualnicbc and dualnicbcha only (suites that exercise PARENT_DATA_SET queries via the BC node).
• No failures are genuinely suite-specific; the dualfollower and tgm failures are pure downstream consequences of the shared GM not locking.


Actionable Recommendations:


1. Re-run the job and inspect GM GNSS hardware first. Confirm whether the WPC T-GM node achieves GNSS/DPLL lock in a fresh run. If it fails again, check the GNSS antenna cable, receiver status, and ts2phc DPLL netlink events on that node. This single fix would resolve ~20/25 failures.

2. Fix pmc invocation to pass GET PARENT_DATA_SET as a single quoted argument. In both the test helper (ptp-operator-conformance-test) and any exec.Command / shell call in linuxptp-daemon, ensure the action+management-ID are passed as one argument string (e.g. exec.Command("pmc", ..., "GET PARENT_DATA_SET")), or switch to the interactive RunPMCExpGetParentDS path with lowercase command ("get PARENT_DATA_SET") to match the RHCOS 10.2 pmc binary's expected input. This resolves the remaining 5 failures.

3. Add a pre-flight GM-lock gate to the CI job. Before running any BC/slave/follower suite, assert that the T-GM node has clock class ≤ 7 (LOCKED); fail fast with a clear infra error rather than letting all 4 suites time out. This would surface the GNSS issue immediately and save ~30 min of wasted CI time per run.


Overall Issue Class: test_flake (dominant: GNSS hardware failure is a known lab environmental flake) + test_regression (secondary: pmc argument bug introduced with RHCOS 10.2 / linuxptp upstream sync 0459bc0)


Overall Severity: CRITICAL — 25/25 failures, 4/4 suites completely down in a nightly periodic job.




Per-Suite Details



dualfollower



Suite: dualfollower (3 failures)


Suite TL;DR: All 3 dualfollower ClockSync tests timed out waiting for node cnfdf31's follower interfaces to reach LOCKED state because the openshift_ptp_clock_state metric for ens2f0np0 was never emitted by linuxptp-daemon, and ens2f3np3 was stuck in LISTENING rather than SLAVE role.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: Slave node never locked — missing clock_state metric and port stuck in LISTENING (affects: all 3 tests)
• Evidence:

```

Tests 1 & 2 — identical error

no Follower port in expected LOCKED state for ptpconfig test-slave1
